interactions.summary: Jiang explains the first Penelope-Odysseus conversation as simultaneous public and private language: servants and suitors hear only a stranger proving knowledge, while Penelope's spirit recognizes Odysseus through the secret of the brooch and the shared memory it carries.
A student reads the Odyssey passage where the disguised Odysseus describes the cloak, brooch, and herald Eurybates, ending with Penelope moved to tears.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization switches to SPEAKER_00 for the quoted reading; Jiang prompts the reading in adjacent context but the focus material is not a public question.

claims.claim: Penelope and Odysseus converse on three levels at once: soul-level mutual recognition, spirit-level longing and fear, and mind-level danger because identity disclosure could get them killed.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive model stated in the March 11, 2026 lecture.

claims.claim: Their dialogue must operate as both public language and private language because hostile servants and suitor spies may overhear them.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive claim stated in the March 11, 2026 lecture.

claims.claim: The brooch is Jiang's hidden sign in the passage: to outsiders it is descriptive ornament, but to Penelope it recalls the departure promise and proves the stranger is Odysseus.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive claim stated in the March 11, 2026 lecture.

claims.claim: The word about the brooch resurrects Penelope's spirit: her mind remains stuck in present danger, but her soul always knew and her heart now knows Odysseus is alive.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive claim stated in the March 11, 2026 lecture.

claims.claim: Once Penelope's spirit acknowledges Odysseus, the two can coordinate the bow contest that will expose Odysseus and enable the killing of the suitors.
claims.temporal_scope: Narrative sequence interpreted in the March 11, 2026 lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The lovers speak in public and private at the same time, because the room is full of enemies.
signature_moments.source_phrase: there's a public public language and there's a private language
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns intimacy into a survival technology, not merely emotional closeness.

signature_moments.moment: One word, the golden brooch, carries twenty years of a marriage back into the room.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The brooch is the lecture's clearest instance of Jiang's secret-language theory.

signature_moments.moment: Penelope's mind is trapped in the present, but her soul has always known.
signature_moments.source_phrase: not the mind... her soul has always known
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses Jiang's three-plane reading into an emotional turn in the poem.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A single exchange that outsiders can hear as ordinary description while lovers hear as coded intimate recognition.
Jiang's name for the secret sign that stores the memory of Odysseus and Penelope's departure promise.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture reconstructs the brooch memory as a twenty-year-old departure scene: ten years in Troy and ten years lost at sea.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders 'brooch' as 'broach' and contains damaged phrases in the quoted passage; extraction follows Jiang's explanation that the object is the golden brooch.
